title: Twill weave on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: Creating a 2/2 twill diagonal using a pick-up stick
excerpt: A rigid heddle normally produces plain weave only, but a pick-up stick inserted between the heddle and the back beam creates a third shed. Alternating the plain weave sheds with the pick-up shed gives a 2/2 twill with a clearly visible diagonal line.
sourceNotes: Pick-up stick techniques for creating twill on a rigid heddle loom are described in rigid heddle weaving texts and magazine publications. The 2/2 twill pick-up sequence is standard. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A weave structure in which the weft passes over and under two or more warp threads in a staggered sequence, creating a diagonal line across the fabric surface. Denim is a twill.
A flat stick woven selectively through the warp threads to create an additional shed beyond those produced by the heddle. Used on rigid heddle looms to create pattern weaves.
A section of weft (or warp) thread that passes over two or more threads without interlacing. Twill is made up of organised floats that shift by one thread on each successive row.
On a rigid heddle with a pick-up stick, the three sheds are: heddle up, pick-up stick on edge, and heddle down. The pick-up stick shed is the third position and produces the twill diagonal.

content.text: A rigid heddle gives two sheds: heddle up and heddle down, producing plain weave. A
adds a third shed by selectively lifting slot threads that the heddle normally leaves in a neutral position. The
over two warp threads and under two.

Warp the loom as normal for plain weave. Lower the heddle to the down position. Working from the right side, weave the pick-up stick through the warp behind the heddle: go under the first slot thread, over the next hole thread (which is now accessible behind the heddle in the down position), under the next slot thread, over the next hole thread, and repeat across the full width. Leave the pick-up stick lying flat behind the heddle when not in use.
Weave the following sequence, repeating it continuously to produce the twill diagonal:
Pick 1: Heddle up. Pass the shuttle right to left.
Pick 2: Pick-up stick on edge (turn the stick on its edge behind the heddle, leave heddle in neutral or slightly up to clear). Pass the shuttle left to right.
Pick 3: Heddle down. Pass the shuttle right to left.
Return to pick 1. Beat after each pick. After 6-8 rows, you should see a clear diagonal line developing across the fabric.

Twill on a rigid heddle works best with smooth yarns in a close-to-balanced sett. The 2/2 floats are visible and attractive in wool or cotton at 8-10 ends per 2.5 cm. Very open setts produce loose floats that catch on things; very close setts produce a fabric so dense that the diagonal is not visible.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to pick-up twill on a rigid heddle.

items.symptom: The diagonal line is not clear or consistent
items.cause: The three-shed sequence was not followed in the correct order, or the pick-up stick was turned at the wrong moment.
items.fix: Mark the side of the fabric where each pick starts so you can check the sequence. Write the three picks on a card taped to the loom until the sequence is memorised.

items.symptom: Pick-up stick is very hard to turn on edge
items.cause: The pick-up stick is too thick for the warp sett, or it was not woven correctly over alternating threads.
items.fix: Use a thinner stick. Re-check that the pick-up stick alternates correctly: under slot thread, over hole thread, across the full width.
